title: University Microeconomics Elasticity Study Guide
subject: Microeconomics
prerequisites: Understand demand curves and price changes.
materials: Course notes on demand and revenue.
summary: A compact study guide for price elasticity of demand and revenue interpretation.
learning_objectives: Interpret price elasticity values in economic context.
Connect elasticity estimates to total revenue decisions.
curriculum_references: Introductory Microeconomics: price elasticity of demand
Introductory Microeconomics: elasticity and total revenue

items.prompt: Explain what it means when price elasticity of demand is less than 1 in absolute value.
items.answer: Demand is price inelastic: quantity demanded changes proportionally less than price.
items.explanation: Inelastic demand usually means total revenue moves in the same direction as price.

items.prompt: Price rises from 10 to 12, and quantity demanded falls from 100 to 90. Use the midpoint method to calculate elasticity.
items.explanation: Percentage quantity change is -10/95. Percentage price change is 2/11. The ratio is about -0.58 depending on rounding; the key interpretation is inelastic.

items.prompt: Why might a medicine with few substitutes have inelastic demand?
items.answer: Consumers may continue buying it despite price changes because it is necessary and substitutes are limited.
items.explanation: Necessity and lack of substitutes are common causes of inelastic demand.
